html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Seventeen — Part Three

The Book of Yesha'yahu

YESHA'YAHU

The Book of Isaiah — 1QIsa-a Great Isaiah Scroll

Chapters 28 through 41

Woes on Yehudah — Sanheriv's Defeat — Khizkiyahu — Comfort My People — Fear Not

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

1QIsa-a — The Great Isaiah Scroll — Cave 1 Qumran

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTERS 28 THROUGH 41 — THE KHIZKIYAHU NARRATIVE AND THE BOOK OF CONSOLATION BEGINS

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 28 Woe to the Drunkards of Efrayim — A Covenant With Death — The Cornerstone in Tziyon

1 Ah, the proud crown of the drunkards of Efrayim, and the fading flower of its glorious beauty, which is on the head of the rich valley of those overcome with wine.

2 Behold, YHWH has one who is mighty and strong — like a storm of hail, a destroying tempest, like a storm of mighty, overflowing waters, he casts down to the earth with his hand.

3 The proud crown of the drunkards of Efrayim will be trodden underfoot.

4 And the fading flower of its glorious beauty, which is on the head of the rich valley, will be like a first-ripe fig before the summer — when someone sees it, he swallows it as soon as it is in his hand.

5 In that day YHWH of hosts will be a crown of glory, and a diadem of beauty, to the remnant of his people,

6 And a spirit of justice to him who sits in judgment, and strength to those who turn back the battle at the gate.

7 These also reel with wine and stagger with strong drink — the priest and the prophet reel with strong drink, they are swallowed by wine, they stagger with strong drink, they reel in vision, they stumble in giving judgment.

8 For all tables are full of filthy vomit, with no space left.

9 To whom will he teach knowledge, and to whom will he explain the message? Those who are weaned from the milk, those taken from the breast?

10 For it is precept upon precept, precept upon precept, line upon line, line upon line, here a little, there a little.

11 For by people of strange lips and with a foreign tongue YHWH will speak to this people,

12 To whom he has said, This is rest — give rest to the weary, and this is repose — yet they would not hear.

13 And the word of YHWH will be to them precept upon precept, precept upon precept, line upon line, line upon line, here a little, there a little, that they may go, and fall backward, and be broken, and snared, and taken.

14 Therefore hear the word of YHWH, you scoffers, who rule this people in Yerushalayim.

15 Because you have said, We have made a covenant with death, and with Sheol we have an agreement. When the overwhelming whip passes through, it will not come to us, for we have made lies our refuge, and in falsehood we have taken shelter.

16 Therefore thus says the Lord YHWH: Behold, I am the one who has laid as a foundation in Tziyon a stone, a tested stone, a precious cornerstone, of a sure foundation: Whoever believes will not be in haste.

17 And I will make justice the line, and righteousness the plumb line. And hail will sweep away the refuge of lies, and waters will overwhelm the shelter.

18 Then your covenant with death will be annulled, and your agreement with Sheol will not stand. When the overwhelming scourge passes through, you will be beaten down by it.

19 As often as it passes through it will take you — for morning by morning it will pass through, by day and by night — and it will be sheer terror to understand the message.

20 For the bed is too short to stretch oneself on, and the covering too narrow to wrap oneself in.

21 For YHWH will rise up as on Mount Perazim, he will be roused as in the Valley of Giv'on — to do his deed, strange is his deed — and to work his work, alien is his work.

22 Now therefore do not scoff, lest your bonds be made strong — for I have heard a decree of destruction from the Lord YHWH of hosts against the whole land.

23 Give ear, and hear my voice — give attention, and hear my speech.

24 Does he who plows for sowing plow continually? Does he continually open and harrow his ground?

25 When he has leveled its surface, does he not scatter dill, sow cumin, and put in wheat in rows and barley in its proper place, and emmer as the border?

26 For he is rightly instructed — his Creator teaches him.

27 Dill is not threshed with a threshing sledge, nor is a cart wheel rolled over cumin — but dill is beaten out with a stick, and cumin with a rod.

28 Does one crush grain for bread? No, he does not thresh it forever. When he drives his cart wheel over it with his horses, he does not crush it.

29 This also comes from YHWH of hosts — he is wonderful in counsel and excellent in wisdom.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 28 in full. Verse 16 — behold, I am the one who has laid as a foundation in Tziyon a stone, a tested stone, a precious cornerstone, of a sure foundation — whoever believes will not be in haste — is one of the most cited verses from Yesha'yahu in the later covenant tradition. 1QIsa-a preserves the cornerstone declaration clearly.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 28 in full. Verse 15 — we have made a covenant with death, and with Sheol we have an agreement — is read as the ultimate covenant perversion: making a covenant not with YHWH but with death itself. The rulers of Yerushalayim have substituted the covenant of death for the covenant of life.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 28. Verse 29 — this also comes from YHWH of hosts — he is wonderful in counsel and excellent in wisdom — closes the farmer parable with the covenant declaration that the wisdom of agricultural seasons reflects the wisdom of YHWH's covenant dealings.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 15-16 — you have made a covenant with death — but I have laid as a foundation in Tziyon a precious cornerstone — whoever believes will not be in haste. The covenant counter-offer to the covenant with death is the cornerstone in Tziyon. The rulers chose death as their covenant partner because they trusted in political alliances with Egypt and Assyria. YHWH offers an alternative covenant foundation — not a foreign alliance but YHWH's own tested stone in Tziyon. Whoever believes — the covenant condition — will not be in haste — the covenant promise of stability.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 29 Woe to Ariel — Their Hearts Far From Me — The Deaf Shall Hear and the Blind Shall See

1 Ah, Ariel, Ariel, the city where Dawid encamped. Add year to year — let the feasts run their round.

2 Yet I will distress Ariel, and there will be moaning and lamentation, and she will be to me like an Ariel.

3 And I will encamp against you all around, and will besiege you with towers and I will raise siegeworks against you.

4 And you will be brought low — from the earth you shall speak, and from the dust your speech will be bowed down. Your voice shall come from the ground like the voice of a ghost, and from the dust your speech shall whisper.

5 But the multitude of your foreign foes shall be like small dust, and the multitude of the ruthless like passing chaff. And in an instant, suddenly,

6 You will be visited by YHWH of hosts with thunder and with earthquake and great noise, with whirlwind and tempest, and the flame of a devouring fire.

7 And the multitude of all the nations that fight against Ariel, all that fight against her and her stronghold and distress her, shall be like a dream, a vision of the night.

8 As when a hungry man dreams, and behold, he is eating, and awakes with his hunger not satisfied, or as when a thirsty man dreams, and behold, he is drinking, and awakes faint, with his thirst not quenched — so shall the multitude of all the nations be that fight against Mount Tziyon.

9 Astonish yourselves and be astonished — blind yourselves and be blind. Be drunk, but not with wine — stagger, but not with strong drink.

10 For YHWH has poured out upon you a spirit of deep sleep, and has closed your eyes — the prophets, and your heads, the seers, he has covered.

11 And the vision of all this has become to you like the words of a book that is sealed. When men give it to one who can read, saying, Read this, he says, I cannot, for it is sealed.

12 And when they give the book to one who cannot read, saying, Read this, he says, I cannot read.

13 And the Lord said: Because this people draw near with their mouth and honor me with their lips, while their hearts are far from me, and their fear of me is a commandment taught by men,

14 Therefore, behold, I will again do wonderful things with this people, with wonder upon wonder — and the wisdom of their wise men shall perish, and the discernment of their discerning men shall be hidden.

15 Ah, you who hide deep from YHWH your counsel, whose deeds are in the dark, and who say, Who sees us? Who knows us?

16 You turn things upside down. Shall the potter be regarded as the clay, that the thing made should say of its maker, He did not make me, or the thing formed say of him who formed it, He has no understanding?

17 Is it not yet a very little while until Lebanon shall be turned into a fruitful field, and the fruitful field shall be regarded as a forest?

18 In that day the deaf shall hear the words of a book, and out of their gloom and darkness the eyes of the blind shall see.

19 The meek shall obtain fresh joy in YHWH, and the poor among mankind shall exult in the Holy One of Yisra'EL.

20 For the ruthless shall come to nothing and the scoffer cease, and all who watch to do evil shall be cut off —

21 Who by a word make a man out to be an offender, and lay a snare for him who reproves in the gate, and with an empty plea turn aside him who is in the right.

22 Therefore thus says YHWH, who redeemed Avraham, concerning the house of Yaakov: Yaakov shall no more be ashamed, no more shall his face grow pale.

23 For when he sees his children, the work of my hands, in his midst, they will sanctify my name — they will sanctify the Holy One of Yaakov and will stand in awe of the Creator of Yisra'EL.

24 And those who go astray in spirit will come to understanding, and those who murmur will accept instruction.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 29 in full. Verse 13 — this people draw near with their mouth and honor me with their lips, while their hearts are far from me, and their fear of me is a commandment taught by men — is one of the most theologically precise diagnoses of religious formalism in the prophetic corpus. 1QIsa-a preserves the verse clearly.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 29 in full. Verse 18-19 — the deaf shall hear, the blind shall see, the meek shall obtain fresh joy — is read as the covenant reversal oracle: what was impossible under judgment becomes possible in covenant restoration. The categories reversed by sin — hearing, seeing, joy — are restored by YHWH's covenant action.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 29. Verse 16 — shall the potter be regarded as the clay — the thing made say of its maker, he did not make me? — is the covenant logical impossibility of denying YHWH's sovereignty. The created thing cannot coherently deny or critique its creator.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 13 — their hearts are far from me, and their fear of me is a commandment taught by men. The covenant critique is not irreligion but wrong religion: they worship, they draw near, they honor — but with lips only, while the heart is elsewhere. The fear of YHWH has become a cultural inheritance rather than a living covenant relationship. This is the most dangerous form of covenant violation in the prophetic diagnosis: not open rebellion but empty compliance.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 30 Woe to the Rebellious Children — Egypt's Help Is Worthless — Write It in a Book — YHWH Waits to Be Gracious

1 Ah, stubborn children, declares YHWH, who carry out a plan, but not mine, and who make an alliance, but not of my Spirit, that they may add sin to sin.

2 Who set out to go down to Egypt, without asking for my direction, to take refuge in the protection of Pharaoh and to seek shelter in the shadow of Egypt.

3 Therefore the protection of Pharaoh shall become your shame, and the shelter in the shadow of Egypt your humiliation.

4 For though his officials are at Tzoan and his envoys reach Khanes,

5 Everyone comes to shame through a people that cannot profit them, that brings neither help nor profit, but shame and disgrace.

6 An oracle on the beasts of the Negev. Through a land of trouble and anguish, from where come the lioness and the lion, the adder and the flying fiery serpent, they carry their riches on the backs of donkeys, and their treasures on the humps of camels, to a people that cannot profit them.

7 Egypt's help is worthless and empty — therefore I have called her Rahav who sits still.

8 And now, go, write it before them on a tablet and inscribe it in a book, that it may be for the time to come as a witness forever.

9 For they are a rebellious people, lying children, children unwilling to hear the instruction of YHWH.

10 Who say to the seers, Do not see, and to the prophets, Do not prophesy to us what is right. Speak to us smooth things — prophesy illusions.

11 Leave the way, turn aside from the path, let us hear no more about the Holy One of Yisra'EL.

12 Therefore thus says the Holy One of Yisra'EL: Because you despise this word and trust in oppression and perverseness and rely on them,

13 Therefore this iniquity shall be to you like a breach in a high wall, bulging out, and about to collapse, whose breaking comes suddenly, in an instant.

14 And its breaking is like that of a potter's vessel that is smashed so ruthlessly that among its fragments not a shard is found with which to take fire from the hearth, or to dip up water out of the cistern.

15 For thus said the Lord YHWH, the Holy One of Yisra'EL: In returning and rest you shall be saved — in quietness and in trust shall be your strength. But you were unwilling,

16 And you said, No. We will flee upon horses — therefore you shall flee away. And, We will ride upon swift steeds — therefore your pursuers shall be swift.

17 A thousand shall flee at the threat of one — at the threat of five you shall flee, till you are left like a flagstaff on the top of a mountain, like a signal on a hill.

18 Therefore YHWH waits to be gracious to you, and therefore he exalts himself to show mercy to you. For YHWH is a Creator of justice — blessed are all those who wait for him.

19 For a people shall dwell in Tziyon, in Yerushalayim. You shall weep no more. He will surely be gracious to you at the sound of your cry. When he hears it, he will answer you.

20 And though YHWH give you the bread of adversity and the water of affliction, yet your Teacher will not hide himself anymore, but your eyes shall see your Teacher.

21 And your ears shall hear a word behind you, saying, This is the way, walk in it, when you turn to the right or when you turn to the left.

22 Then you will defile your carved idols overlaid with silver and your gold-plated metal images. You will scatter them as unclean things. You will say to them, Be gone.

23 And he will give rain for the seed with which you sow the ground, and bread, the produce of the ground, which will be rich and plenteous. In that day your livestock will graze in large pastures.

24 And the oxen and the donkeys that work the ground will eat seasoned fodder, which has been winnowed with shovel and fork.

25 And on every lofty mountain and every high hill there will be brooks running with water, in the day of the great slaughter, when the towers fall.

26 Moreover, the light of the moon will be as the light of the sun, and the light of the sun will be sevenfold, as the light of seven days, in the day when YHWH binds up the brokenness of his people, and heals the wounds inflicted by his blow.

27 Behold, the name of YHWH comes from far, burning with his anger, and in thick rising smoke. His lips are full of fury, and his tongue is like a devouring fire.

28 His breath is like an overflowing stream that reaches up to the neck, to sift the nations with the sieve of destruction, and to place on the jaws of the peoples a bridle that leads them astray.

29 You shall have a song as in the night when a holy feast is kept, and gladness of heart, as when one sets out to the sound of the flute to go to the mountain of YHWH, to the Rock of Yisra'EL.

30 And YHWH will cause his majestic voice to be heard and the descending blow of his arm to be seen, in furious anger and a flame of devouring fire, with a cloudburst and storm and hailstones.

31 The Assyrians will be terror-stricken at the voice of YHWH, when he strikes with his rod.

32 And every stroke of the appointed staff that YHWH lays on them will be to the sound of tambourines and lyres. Battling with brandished arm, he will fight them.

33 For a burning place has long been prepared — it is made ready for the king also. Its pyre is made deep and wide, with fire and wood in abundance. The breath of YHWH, like a stream of sulfur, kindles it.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 30 in full. Verse 15 — in returning and rest you shall be saved — in quietness and in trust shall be your strength — but you were unwilling — is one of the most concentrated covenant summaries in the prophetic corpus. 1QIsa-a preserves the contrast clearly: the covenant way of salvation — return, rest, quietness, trust — rejected in favor of horses and swift steeds.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 30 in full. Verse 18 — YHWH waits to be gracious to you — he exalts himself to show mercy to you — for YHWH is a Creator of justice — blessed are all those who wait for him — is read as the covenant declaration that YHWH's patient waiting for Yisra'EL mirrors the covenant posture he asks of Yisra'EL toward him.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 30. Verse 10-11 — speak to us smooth things — prophesy illusions — let us hear no more about the Holy One of Yisra'EL. The covenant community's demand for comfortable prophecy over covenant truth is the specific form of covenant deafness that produces the blindness of Chapter 29.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 15 — in returning and rest you shall be saved — in quietness and in trust shall be your strength — but you were unwilling. The covenant way of salvation offered by YHWH is the exact opposite of what the political leadership chose. They fled on horses. They sought swift steeds. They went to Egypt. YHWH offered returning — the covenant act of teshuvah — rest, quietness, and trust. These are not passivity — they are the active posture of the covenant community that places its trust entirely in YHWH rather than in political and military power. The refusal — but you were unwilling — is the covenant tragedy.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 31 Woe to Those Who Go Down to Egypt — YHWH Will Protect Yerushalayim Like Birds Hovering

1 Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help and rely on horses, who trust in chariots because they are many and in horsemen because they are very strong, but do not look to the Holy One of Yisra'EL or consult YHWH.

2 And yet he is wise and brings disaster — he does not call back his words, but will arise against the house of the evildoers and against the helpers of those who work iniquity.

3 The Egyptians are man, and not Creator, and their horses are flesh, and not spirit. When YHWH stretches out his hand, the helper will stumble, and he who is helped will fall, and they will all perish together.

4 For thus YHWH said to me, As a lion or a young lion growls over his prey, and when a band of shepherds is called out against him he is not terrified by their shouting or daunted at their noise, so YHWH of hosts will come down to fight on Mount Tziyon and on its hill.

5 Like birds hovering, so YHWH of hosts will protect Yerushalayim — he will protect and deliver it — he will spare and rescue it.

6 Turn to him from whom people have deeply revolted, O children of Yisra'EL.

7 For in that day everyone shall cast away his idols of silver and his idols of gold, which your hands have sinfully made for you.

8 And the Assyrian shall fall by a sword, not of man, and a sword, not of man, shall devour him, and he shall flee from the sword, and his young men shall be put to forced labor.

9 His rock shall pass away in terror, and his officers desert the standard in panic, declares YHWH, whose fire is in Tziyon, and whose furnace is in Yerushalayim.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 31 in full. Verse 3 — the Egyptians are man and not Creator, and their horses are flesh and not spirit — is the covenant definition of the alliance with Egypt: choosing flesh over spirit, man over Creator. Verse 8 — the Assyrian shall fall by a sword not of man — is the covenant declaration fulfilled in Melakhim Bet 19:35 when the angel strikes 185,000 in a single night.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 31 in full. Verse 5 — like birds hovering, so YHWH of hosts will protect Yerushalayim — he will protect and deliver it, spare and rescue it — is read as the covenant image of divine maternal protection: the hovering bird over its nest echoes Devarim 32:11 — as an eagle stirs up its nest and hovers over its young.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 31. Verse 8-9 — the Assyrian shall fall by a sword not of man — is the covenant declaration that makes the fulfillment of Melakhim Bet 19:35 recognizable as covenant prophecy: no human army killed 185,000 Assyrians. A sword not of man — a divine sword — executed the judgment.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — woe to those who go down to Egypt for help and rely on horses and trust in chariots — but do not look to the Holy One of Yisra'EL or consult YHWH. The covenant critique is not military incompetence — Egypt's horses and chariots were genuinely impressive. The critique is covenant misplacement of trust: horses are real and powerful, but they are flesh and not spirit — verse 3. The covenant community's reliance on visible military power rather than invisible covenant protection is not stupidity but covenant blindness.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 32 A King Will Reign in Righteousness — The Women of Yehudah — The Spirit Poured From on High

1 Behold, a king will reign in righteousness, and princes will rule in justice.

2 Each will be like a hiding place from the wind, a shelter from the storm, like streams of water in a dry place, like the shade of a great rock in a weary land.

3 Then the eyes of those who see will not be closed, and the ears of those who hear will give attention.

4 The heart of the hasty will understand and know, and the tongue of the stammerers will hasten to speak distinctly.

5 The fool will no more be called noble, nor the scoundrel said to be honorable.

6 For the fool speaks folly, and his heart is busy with iniquity, to practice ungodliness, to utter error concerning YHWH, to leave the craving of the hungry unsatisfied, and to deprive the thirsty of drink.

7 As for the scoundrel — his devices are evil. He plans wicked schemes to ruin the poor with lying words, even when the plea of the needy is right.

8 But he who is noble plans noble things, and on noble things he stands.

9 Rise up, you women who are at ease — hear my voice — you complacent daughters — give ear to my speech.

10 In little more than a year you will shudder, you complacent women — for the grape harvest fails, the fruit harvest will not come.

11 Tremble, you women who are at ease — shudder, you complacent ones. Strip, and make yourselves bare, and tie sackcloth around your waist.

12 Beat your breasts for the pleasant fields, for the fruitful vine,

13 For the soil of my people growing up in thorns and briers, yes, for all the joyous houses in the exultant city.

14 For the palace is forsaken, the populous city deserted. The hill and the watchtower will become dens forever, a joy of wild donkeys, a pasture of flocks,

15 Until the Spirit is poured upon us from on high, and the wilderness becomes a fruitful field, and the fruitful field is deemed a forest.

16 Then justice will dwell in the wilderness, and righteousness abide in the fruitful field.

17 And the effect of righteousness will be peace, and the result of righteousness, quietness and trust forever.

18 My people will abide in a peaceful habitation, in secure dwellings, and in quiet resting places.

19 And it will hail when the forest falls down, and the city will be utterly laid low.

20 Happy are you who sow beside all waters, who let the feet of the ox and the donkey range free.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 32 in full. Verse 15 — until the Spirit is poured upon us from on high, and the wilderness becomes a fruitful field — is the covenant turning point of the oracle: the restoration of justice and peace depends not on political reform but on the pouring out of the Spirit from on high. The covenant transformation is pneumatic — Spirit-initiated.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 32 in full. Verse 17 — the effect of righteousness will be peace, and the result of righteousness, quietness and trust forever — is read as the covenant chain of consequences: tzedakah produces shalom, shalom produces betakh — security and trust. The covenant social order flows from the covenant moral order.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 32. Verse 1 — a king will reign in righteousness, and princes will rule in justice — opens the chapter with the covenant vision of ideal governance. The king who reigns in righteousness is the covenant counter-image to every king in Melakhim who did evil in YHWH's sight.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 15-17 — until the Spirit is poured upon us from on high — then justice will dwell in the wilderness — the effect of righteousness will be peace. The covenant sequence is clear: Spirit poured — righteousness established — peace as the fruit. The social transformation Yesha'yahu prophesies is not achieved through better laws or improved political structures but through the covenant gift of the Spirit from on high. Human righteousness is insufficient to produce covenant peace. The Spirit-initiated righteousness of verse 15 produces a covenant peace that is qualitatively different from political stability.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 33 Woe to You Destroyer — YHWH Is Our Judge — Tziyon the Quiet Habitation — Your Eyes Will See the King

1 Ah, you destroyer, who yourself have not been destroyed — you traitor, whom none has betrayed. When you have ceased to destroy, you will be destroyed. And when you have finished betraying, they will betray you.

2 O YHWH, be gracious to us — we wait for you. Be our arm every morning, our salvation in the time of trouble.

3 At the tumultuous noise peoples flee — when you lift yourself up, nations are scattered.

4 And your spoil is gathered as the caterpillar gathers — as locusts leap, it is leaped upon.

5 YHWH is exalted, for he dwells on high. He will fill Tziyon with justice and righteousness.

6 And he will be the stability of your times, abundance of salvation, wisdom, and knowledge — the fear of YHWH is Tziyon's treasure.

7 Behold, their heroes cry in the streets, the envoys of peace weep bitterly.

8 The highways lie waste, the wayfaring man ceases. Covenants are broken, witnesses are despised, there is no regard for man.

9 The land mourns and languishes — Lebanon is confounded and withers away. The Sharon is like a desert, and Bashan and Karmel shake off their leaves.

10 Now I will arise, says YHWH — now I will lift myself up — now I will be exalted.

11 You conceive chaff, you give birth to stubble. Your breath is a fire that will consume you.

12 And the peoples will be as if burned to lime, like thorns cut down, that are burned in the fire.

13 Hear, you who are far off, what I have done — and you who are near, acknowledge my might.

14 The sinners in Tziyon are afraid — trembling has seized the godless: Who among us can dwell with the devouring fire? Who among us can dwell with everlasting burnings?

15 He who walks righteously and speaks uprightly, who despises the gain of oppressions, who shakes his hands, lest they hold a bribe, who stops his ears from hearing of bloodshed and shuts his eyes from looking on evil —

16 He will dwell on the heights — his place of defense will be the fortresses of rocks. His bread will be given him, his water will be sure.

17 Your eyes will see the king in his beauty — they will behold a land that stretches afar.

18 Your heart will muse on the terror: Where is he who counted, where is he who weighed the tribute? Where is he who counted the towers?

19 You will see no more the insolent people, the people of an obscure speech that you cannot comprehend, stammering in a tongue that you cannot understand.

20 Behold Tziyon, the city of our appointed feasts. Your eyes will see Yerushalayim, an untroubled habitation, an immovable tent, whose stakes will never be plucked up, nor will any of its cords be broken.

21 But there YHWH in majesty will be for us a place of broad rivers and streams, where no galley with oars can go, nor majestic ship can pass.

22 For YHWH is our judge, YHWH is our lawgiver, YHWH is our king — he will save us.

23 Your ropes hang loose — they cannot hold the mast firm in its place or keep the sail spread out. Then prey and spoil in abundance will be divided — even the lame will take the prey.

24 And no inhabitant will say, I am sick — the people who dwell there will be forgiven their iniquity.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 33 in full. Verse 22 — YHWH is our judge, YHWH is our lawgiver, YHWH is our king — he will save us — is the covenant declaration of YHWH's threefold office: judicial, legislative, royal. All three functions that human kings claimed are declared to belong exclusively to YHWH in the covenant restoration.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 33 in full. Verse 17 — your eyes will see the king in his beauty — they will behold a land that stretches afar — is read as the covenant vision of the restored covenant king seen in his full glory, in contrast to the blind and deaf covenant community of the judgment sections.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 33. Verse 6 — the fear of YHWH is Tziyon's treasure — is the covenant declaration that the covenant community's most valuable possession is not military strength, political alliances, or material wealth but the covenant orientation of fear toward YHWH.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 14-16 — who among us can dwell with the devouring fire? He who walks righteously and speaks uprightly — he will dwell on the heights. The covenant community in Tziyon — the holy city — asks the right question when YHWH's presence comes: who can dwell with this fire? The answer is covenant ethics enumerated precisely: righteousness, upright speech, rejection of oppressive gain, refusal of bribes, stopping the ears from bloodshed, shutting the eyes from evil. The covenant life that qualifies for YHWH's presence is not ritual but ethical.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 34 YHWH's Indignation on All the Nations — Edom Reduced to Ruin — The Land of Jackals and Desolation

1 Draw near, O nations, to hear, and give attention, O peoples. Let the earth hear, and all that fills it — the world, and all that comes from it.

2 For YHWH is enraged against all the nations, and furious against all their host. He has devoted them to destruction, has given them over for slaughter.

3 Their slain shall be cast out, and the stench of their corpses shall rise — the mountains shall flow with their blood.

4 All the host of heaven shall rot away, and the skies roll up like a scroll. All their host shall fall, as leaves fall from the vine, like leaves falling from the fig tree.

5 For my sword has drunk its fill in the heavens — behold, it descends for judgment upon Edom, upon the people I have devoted to destruction.

6 YHWH has a sword — it is sated with blood, it is gorged with fat, with the blood of lambs and goats, with the fat of the kidneys of rams. For YHWH has a sacrifice in Botzrah, a great slaughter in the land of Edom.

7 Wild oxen shall fall with them, and young steers with the mighty bulls. Their land shall drink its fill of blood, and their soil shall be gorged with fat.

8 For YHWH has a day of vengeance, a year of recompense for the cause of Tziyon.

9 And the streams of Edom shall be turned into pitch, and her soil into sulfur — her land shall become burning pitch.

10 Night and day it shall not be quenched — its smoke shall go up forever. From generation to generation it shall lie waste — none shall pass through it forever and ever.

11 But the hawk and the porcupine shall possess it — the owl and the raven shall dwell in it. He shall stretch the line of confusion over it, and the plumb line of emptiness.

12 Its nobles — there is no one there to call it a kingdom, and all its princes shall be nothing.

13 Thorns shall grow over its strongholds, nettles and thistles in its fortresses. It shall be the haunt of jackals, an abode for ostriches.

14 And wild animals shall meet with hyenas, the wild goat shall cry to his fellow — indeed, there the night bird settles and finds for herself a resting place.

15 There the owl nests and lays and hatches and gathers her young in her shadow. Indeed, there the hawks are gathered, each one with her mate.

16 Seek and read from the book of YHWH: Not one of these shall be missing — none shall be without her mate. For the mouth of YHWH has commanded, and his Spirit has gathered them.

17 He has cast the lot for them — his hand has portioned it out to them with the line. They shall possess it forever — from generation to generation they shall dwell in it.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 34 in full. The Edom oracle connects to the covenant tradition of YHWH's judgment against Edom throughout the Nevi'im — from Amos to Ovadyah to Yirmeyahu to Maleakhi. Edom functions as the covenant archetype of the persistent enemy of the covenant people.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 34 in full. Verse 16 — seek and read from the book of YHWH — not one of these shall be missing — for the mouth of YHWH has commanded and his Spirit has gathered them — is read as the covenant declaration of prophetic reliability: the covenant word is as reliable as the natural order YHWH commands.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 34. Verse 4 — all the host of heaven shall rot away, and the skies roll up like a scroll — is the cosmic dissolution language of the covenant apocalypse, repeating the imagery of Chapter 24 but applied specifically to the judgment of the nations.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — for YHWH has a day of vengeance, a year of recompense for the cause of Tziyon. The covenant logic of YHWH's judgment of Edom: it is not arbitrary punitive action but covenant justice on behalf of Tziyon. Every covenant oracle against the nations in Yesha'yahu is ultimately covenant advocacy for the covenant people. YHWH's anger against the nations is the expression of his covenant faithfulness toward Yisra'EL.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 35 The Wilderness Shall Rejoice — Streams in the Desert — The Way of Holiness — The Redeemed Shall Return

1 The wilderness and the dry land shall be glad — the desert shall rejoice and blossom like the crocus.

2 It shall blossom abundantly and rejoice with joy and singing. The glory of Lebanon shall be given to it, the majesty of Karmel and Sharon. They shall see the glory of YHWH, the majesty of our Creator.

3 Strengthen the weak hands, and make firm the feeble knees.

4 Say to those who have an anxious heart, Be strong — fear not. Behold, your Creator will come with vengeance, with the recompense of Creator. He will come and save you.

5 Then the eyes of the blind shall be opened, and the ears of the deaf unstopped.

6 Then shall the lame man leap like a deer, and the tongue of the mute sing for joy. For waters break forth in the wilderness, and streams in the desert.

7 The burning sand shall become a pool, and the thirsty ground springs of water. In the haunt of jackals, where they lie down, the grass shall become reeds and rushes.

8 And a highway shall be there, and it shall be called the Way of Holiness. The unclean shall not pass over it. It shall belong to those who walk on the way — even if they are fools, they shall not go astray.

9 No lion shall be there, nor shall any ravenous beast come up on it. They shall not be found there, but the redeemed shall walk there.

10 And the ransomed of YHWH shall return and come to Tziyon with singing. Everlasting joy shall be upon their heads — they shall obtain gladness and joy, and sorrow and sighing shall flee away.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 35 in full. Chapter 35 is the covenant counter-image to Chapter 34: where Edom becomes desolate waste, the covenant land becomes a flowering garden. Verse 8 — the Way of Holiness — the highway through the wilderness — is the covenant road of return from exile, the new Exodus.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 35 in full. Verse 10 — the ransomed of YHWH shall return and come to Tziyon with singing — everlasting joy shall be upon their heads — sorrow and sighing shall flee away — is the covenant climax of the entire first half of Yesha'yahu: the scattered covenant people gathered, the mourning reversed, the covenant joy restored.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 35. Verse 1-2 — the wilderness shall rejoice and blossom — mirrors the vineyard of Chapter 5 that YHWH abandoned to briers and thorns. The covenant reversal: what became wilderness through covenant violation becomes garden through covenant restoration.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5-6 — then the eyes of the blind shall be opened — the ears of the deaf unstopped — the lame shall leap like a deer — the tongue of the mute sing for joy. The covenant restoration reverses each form of covenant disability cataloged in the judgment sections: the blind who would not see, the deaf who would not hear, the lame who could not walk. The covenant healing is comprehensive and specific. Verse 8 — the Way of Holiness — even if they are fools, they shall not go astray. The covenant road of return is so clearly marked that not even fools can miss it. The covenant restoration is not for the spiritually sophisticated but for the scattered and the weak.

THE KHIZKIYAHU NARRATIVE — CHAPTERS 36 THROUGH 39

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 36 Sanheriv's Campaign — The Rabshakeh at the Conduit — Do Not Trust in Khizkiyahu — Has YHWH Sent Me?

1 In the fourteenth year of King Khizkiyahu, Sanheriv king of Assyria came up against all the fortified cities of Yehudah and took them.

2 And the king of Assyria sent the Rabshakeh from Lakhish to King Khizkiyahu at Yerushalayim, with a great army. And he stood by the conduit of the upper pool on the highway to the Washer's Field.

3 And there came out to him Elyakim the son of Khilkiyahu, who was over the household, and Shevna the secretary, and Yo'akh the son of Asaf, the recorder.

4 And the Rabshakeh said to them, Say to Khizkiyahu, Thus says the great king, the king of Assyria: On what do you rest this trust of yours?

5 Do you think that mere words are strategy and power for war? In whom do you now trust, that you have rebelled against me?

6 Behold, you are trusting in Egypt, that broken reed of a staff, which will pierce the hand of any man who leans on it. Such is Pharaoh king of Egypt to all who trust in him.

7 But if you say to me, We trust in YHWH our Creator, is it not he whose high places and altars Khizkiyahu has removed, saying to Yehudah and to Yerushalayim, You shall worship before this altar?

8 Come now, make a wager with my master the king of Assyria: I will give you two thousand horses, if you are able on your part to set riders on them.

9 How then can you repulse a single captain among the least of my master's servants, when you trust in Egypt for chariots and for horsemen?

10 Moreover, is it without YHWH that I have come up against this land to destroy it? YHWH said to me, Go up against this land and destroy it.

11 Then Elyakim and Shevna and Yo'akh said to the Rabshakeh, Please speak to your servants in Aramaic, for we understand it. Do not speak to us in the language of Yehudah within the hearing of the people who are on the wall.

12 But the Rabshakeh said, Has my master sent me to speak these words to your master and to you, and not to the men who are sitting on the wall, who are doomed with you to eat their own dung and drink their own urine?

13 Then the Rabshakeh stood and called out in a loud voice in the language of Yehudah: Hear the words of the great king, the king of Assyria.

14 Thus says the king: Do not let Khizkiyahu deceive you, for he will not be able to deliver you.

15 Do not let Khizkiyahu make you trust in YHWH by saying, YHWH will surely deliver us — this city will not be given into the hand of the king of Assyria.

16 Do not listen to Khizkiyahu. For thus says the king of Assyria: Make your peace with me and come out to me. Then each one of you will eat of his own vine, and each one of his own fig tree, and each one of you will drink the water of his own cistern,

17 Until I come and take you away to a land like your own land, a land of grain and wine, a land of bread and vineyards.

18 Beware lest Khizkiyahu mislead you by saying, YHWH will deliver us. Has any of the gods of the nations delivered his land out of the hand of the king of Assyria?

19 Where are the gods of Khamath and Arpad? Where are the gods of Sefarvaim? Have they delivered Samaria out of my hand?

20 Who among all the gods of these lands have delivered their lands out of my hand, that YHWH should deliver Yerushalayim out of my hand?

21 But they were silent and answered him not a word, for the king's command was, Do not answer him.

22 Then Elyakim the son of Khilkiyahu, who was over the household, and Shevna the secretary, and Yo'akh the son of Asaf, the recorder, came to Khizkiyahu with their clothes torn and told him the words of the Rabshakeh.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapters 36-39 in full. These chapters correspond almost exactly to Melakhim Bet 18:13-20:19, with minor variants. The inclusion of the Khizkiyahu historical narrative in Yesha'yahu connects the prophetic ministry of Yesha'yahu directly to the historical crisis it addressed — and demonstrates that 1QIsa-a preserves both the prophetic and historical traditions as an integrated unit.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 36 in full. The Rabshakeh's public address in the language of Yehudah — verse 13 — specifically targeting the ordinary people on the wall rather than the diplomatic delegation — is read as the covenant strategy of demoralization: bypass the leaders and attack the faith of the people directly.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 36 with minor variants from the Masoretic. The parallel with Melakhim Bet 18 is close. 1QIsa-a has a number of small orthographic differences but no significant textual variants that change the meaning. This compilation follows 1QIsa-a as the primary witness.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10 — YHWH said to me, Go up against this land and destroy it. The Rabshakeh's claim to be on a divine mission is the most dangerous element of the Assyrian theological assault. It places Khizkiyahu in an impossible position: if the Rabshakeh is wrong, resistance is right. If the Rabshakeh is right — if YHWH did commission the Assyrian invasion — then resistance is futile covenant rebellion. Yesha'yahu's oracle in Chapter 37 resolves the question: YHWH did use Assyria — Chapter 10:5 — but Assyria exceeded its commission through pride, and YHWH will now judge Assyria for that excess.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 37 Khizkiyahu's Prayer — Yesha'yahu's Oracle — Do Not Be Afraid — 185,000 Slain by the Angel

1 As soon as King Khizkiyahu heard it, he tore his clothes and covered himself with sackcloth and went into the house of YHWH.

2 And he sent Elyakim, who was over the household, and Shevna the secretary, and the senior priests, covered with sackcloth, to the prophet Yesha'yahu the son of Amoz.

3 They said to him, Thus says Khizkiyahu: This day is a day of distress, of rebuke, and of disgrace — children have come to the point of birth, and there is no strength to bring them forth.

4 It may be that YHWH your Creator will hear the words of the Rabshakeh, whom his master the king of Assyria has sent to mock the living Creator, and will rebuke the words that YHWH your Creator has heard. Therefore lift up your prayer for the remnant that is left.

5 When the servants of King Khizkiyahu came to Yesha'yahu,

6 Yesha'yahu said to them, Say to your master, Thus says YHWH: Do not be afraid because of the words that you have heard, with which the servants of the king of Assyria have reviled me.

7 Behold, I will put a spirit in him, so that he shall hear a rumor and return to his own land, and I will make him fall by the sword in his own land.

8 The Rabshakeh returned, and found the king of Assyria fighting against Livnah, for he had heard that the king had left Lakhish.

9 Now the king heard concerning Tirhakah king of Kush, He has set out to fight against you. And when he heard it, he sent messengers to Khizkiyahu, saying,

10 Thus shall you speak to Khizkiyahu king of Yehudah: Do not let your Creator in whom you trust deceive you by saying, Yerushalayim will not be given into the hand of the king of Assyria.

11 Behold, you have heard what the kings of Assyria have done to all lands, devoting them to destruction. And shall you be delivered?

12 Have the gods of the nations delivered them, the nations that my fathers destroyed, Gozan, Kharan, Retzef, and the people of Eden who were in Telassar?

13 Where is the king of Khamath, the king of Arpad, the king of the city of Sefarvaim, the king of Hena, or the king of Ivvah?

14 Khizkiyahu received the letter from the hand of the messengers and read it. And Khizkiyahu went up to the house of YHWH and spread it before YHWH.

15 And Khizkiyahu prayed to YHWH, saying:

16 O YHWH of hosts, Creator of Yisra'EL, enthroned above the cherubim, you are the Creator, you alone, of all the kingdoms of the earth. You have made heaven and earth.

17 Incline your ear, O YHWH, and hear — open your eyes, O YHWH, and see — and hear all the words of Sanheriv, which he has sent to mock the living Creator.

18 Truly, O YHWH, the kings of Assyria have laid waste all the nations and their lands,

19 And have cast their gods into the fire — for they were not gods, but the work of men's hands, wood and stone. Therefore they were destroyed.

20 So now, O YHWH our Creator, save us from his hand, that all the kingdoms of the earth may know that you alone are YHWH.

21 Then Yesha'yahu the son of Amoz sent to Khizkiyahu, saying, Thus says YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL: Because you have prayed to me concerning Sanheriv king of Assyria,

22 This is the word that YHWH has spoken concerning him: She despises you, she scorns you — the virgin daughter of Tziyon — she wags her head behind you, the daughter of Yerushalayim.

23 Whom have you mocked and reviled? Against whom have you raised your voice and lifted your eyes to the heights? Against the Holy One of Yisra'EL.

24 By your servants you have mocked YHWH, and you have said: With my many chariots I have gone up the heights of the mountains, to the far recesses of Lebanon, to cut down its tallest cedars, its choicest cypresses — I entered its farthest lodging place, its most fruitful forest.

25 I dug wells and drank waters, to dry up with the sole of my foot all the streams of Egypt.

26 Have you not heard that I determined it long ago? I planned from days of old what now I bring to pass — that you should make fortified cities crash into heaps of ruins,

27 While their inhabitants, shorn of strength, are dismayed and confounded, and have become like plants of the field and like tender grass, like grass on the housetops, blighted before it is grown.

28 I know your sitting down and your going out and coming in, and your raging against me.

29 Because you have raged against me and your complacency has come to my ears, I will put my hook in your nose and my bit in your mouth, and I will turn you back on the way by which you came.

30 And this shall be the sign for you: this year eat what grows of itself, and in the second year what springs of the same. Then in the third year sow and reap, and plant vineyards, and eat their fruit.

31 And the surviving remnant of the house of Yehudah shall again take root downward and bear fruit upward.

32 For out of Yerushalayim shall go a remnant, and out of Mount Tziyon a band of survivors. The zeal of YHWH of hosts will do this.

33 Therefore thus says YHWH concerning the king of Assyria: He shall not come into this city or shoot an arrow there or come before it with a shield or cast up a siege mound against it.

34 By the way that he came, by the same he shall return, and he shall not come into this city, declares YHWH.

35 For I will defend this city to save it, for my own sake and for the sake of my servant Dawid.

36 And the angel of YHWH went out and struck down a hundred and eighty-five thousand in the camp of the Assyrians. And when people arose early in the morning, behold, these were all dead bodies.

37 Then Sanheriv king of Assyria departed and returned home and lived at Nineveh.

38 And as he was worshiping in the house of Nisrokh his god, Adrammelekh and Sharezer, his sons, struck him down with the sword. And they escaped into the land of Ararat. And Esarhaddon his son reigned in his place.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 37 in full. The covenant prayer of Khizkiyahu — spreading the Assyrian letter before YHWH — is one of the most significant covenant acts in the prophetic corpus. 1QIsa-a preserves it in full. The theological center of the prayer — verse 16-20 — is the covenant declaration of YHWH's universal sovereignty as the ground for the specific covenant deliverance requested.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 37 in full. Verse 35 — for I will defend this city for my own sake and for the sake of my servant Dawid — is read as the dual covenant ground of YHWH's protection: his own covenant name and the Davidic covenant. Both operate simultaneously as the basis for Yerushalayim's deliverance.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 37 with minor variants from the Masoretic. Verse 36 — the angel of YHWH struck down 185,000 in a single night. The fulfillment of the covenant prophecies of Chapters 31:8 — the Assyrian shall fall by a sword not of man — and 10:12 — YHWH will punish the arrogant heart of the king of Assyria. Every element of the covenant promise is fulfilled precisely.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 14 — Khizkiyahu received the letter and spread it before YHWH. The covenant act of taking the physical threat to YHWH's presence is the pivot on which the entire narrative turns. Akhaz refused to ask a sign from YHWH — Chapter 7:12. Khizkiyahu does not merely ask — he physically places the threat letter before YHWH. The covenant prayer that follows — verse 16-20 — is the most theologically complete prayer in the prophetic corpus: YHWH's universal sovereignty acknowledged, the specific covenant violation by Assyria identified — mocking the living Creator — and the specific covenant request framed for universal covenant purpose: that all the kingdoms of the earth may know that you alone are YHWH.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 38 Khizkiyahu's Illness — Set Your House in Order — The Sun Goes Back — The Writing of Khizkiyahu

1 In those days Khizkiyahu became sick and was at the point of death. And Yesha'yahu the prophet the son of Amoz came to him, and said to him, Thus says YHWH: Set your house in order, for you shall die and not recover.

2 Then Khizkiyahu turned his face to the wall and prayed to YHWH,

3 And said, Please, O YHWH, remember how I have walked before you in faithfulness and with a whole heart, and have done what is good in your sight. And Khizkiyahu wept bitterly.

4 Then the word of YHWH came to Yesha'yahu:

5 Go and say to Khizkiyahu, Thus says YHWH, the Creator of Dawid your father: I have heard your prayer — I have seen your tears. Behold, I will add fifteen years to your life.

6 I will deliver you and this city from the hand of the king of Assyria, and defend this city.

7 This shall be the sign to you from YHWH, that YHWH will do this thing that he has promised:

8 Behold, I will make the shadow cast by the declining sun on the steps of Akhaz turn back ten steps. So the sun turned back on the steps by ten steps.

9 A writing of Khizkiyahu king of Yehudah, after he had been sick and had recovered from his sickness:

10 I said, In the middle of my days I must depart. I am consigned to the gates of Sheol for the rest of my years.

11 I said, I shall not see YHWH, YHWH in the land of the living. I shall look on man no more among the inhabitants of the world.

12 My dwelling is plucked up and removed from me like a shepherd's tent. Like a weaver I have rolled up my life — he cuts me off from the loom. From day to night you bring me to an end.

13 I calmed myself until morning. Like a lion he breaks all my bones. From day to night you bring me to an end.

14 Like a swallow or a crane I chirp — I moan like a dove. My eyes are weary with looking upward. O Lord, I am oppressed — be my pledge of safety.

15 What shall I say? For he has spoken to me, and he himself has done it. I walk slowly all my years because of the bitterness of my soul.

16 O Lord, by these things men live, and in all these is the life of my spirit. Oh, restore me to health and make me live.

17 Behold, it was for my welfare that I had great bitterness. It is you who has held back my life from the pit of destruction. For you have cast all my sins behind your back.

18 For Sheol does not thank you — death does not praise you. Those who go down to the pit do not hope for your faithfulness.

19 The living, the living, he thanks you, as I do this day. The father makes known to the children your faithfulness.

20 YHWH will save me, and we will play my music on stringed instruments all the days of our lives, at the house of YHWH.

21 Now Yesha'yahu had said, Let them take a cake of figs and apply it to the boil, that he may recover.

22 Khizkiyahu also had said, What is the sign that I shall go up to the house of YHWH?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 38 in full. The Writing of Khizkiyahu — verses 9-20 — is a psalm of illness and recovery unique to the Yesha'yahu account. It does not appear in the parallel account in Melakhim Bet 20. 1QIsa-a preserves the full psalm as an integral part of the chapter.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 38 in full. Verse 17 — it was for my welfare that I had great bitterness — for you have cast all my sins behind your back — is read as the covenant declaration of the redemptive function of covenant suffering: the bitterness was not punishment alone but covenant transformation.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a includes the Writing of Khizkiyahu — verses 9-20 — which is absent from the Melakhim Bet parallel. This is one of the significant differences between the Yesha'yahu and Melakhim Bet accounts. 1QIsa-a clearly preserves it as original to Yesha'yahu.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 18-19 — Sheol does not thank you — death does not praise you — but the living, the living, he thanks you. The covenant argument for continued life is the covenant argument of praise: the dead cannot offer covenant praise to YHWH, but the living can. Khizkiyahu's prayer for extended life is grounded in the covenant purpose of praise. His life extension is not for personal pleasure but for covenant testimony: the father makes known to the children your faithfulness — verse 19.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 39 The Babylonian Envoys — Khizkiyahu Shows Them Everything — The Word of YHWH — All Shall Be Carried to Babylon

1 At that time Merodakh-Baladan the son of Baladan, king of Babylon, sent envoys with letters and a present to Khizkiyahu, for he heard that he had been sick and had recovered.

2 And Khizkiyahu welcomed them gladly. And he showed them his treasure house, the silver, the gold, the spices, the precious oil, his whole armory, all that was found in his storehouses. There was nothing in his house or in all his realm that Khizkiyahu did not show them.

3 Then Yesha'yahu the prophet came to King Khizkiyahu, and said to him, What did these men say? And from where did they come to you? Khizkiyahu said, They have come to me from a far country, from Babylon.

4 He said, What have they seen in your house? Khizkiyahu answered, They have seen all that is in my house. There is nothing in my storehouses that I did not show them.

5 Then Yesha'yahu said to Khizkiyahu, Hear the word of YHWH of hosts:

6 Behold, the days are coming, when all that is in your house, and that which your fathers have stored up till this day, shall be carried to Babylon. Nothing shall be left, says YHWH.

7 And some of your own sons, who will come from you, whom you will father, shall be taken away, and they shall be eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon.

8 Then Khizkiyahu said to Yesha'yahu, The word of YHWH that you have spoken is good. And he said, For there will be peace and security in my days.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 39 in full. Chapter 39 is the hinge between the first half of Yesha'yahu — Chapters 1-39, focused on the Assyrian crisis — and the second half — Chapters 40-66, addressing the Babylonian exile. The Babylonian envoy episode plants the seed of the coming exile. Yesha'yahu's announcement of the Babylonian deportation connects the two halves of the book.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 39 in full. Verse 8 — the word of YHWH that you have spoken is good — for there will be peace and security in my days — is read as the most troubling response in the Khizkiyahu narrative. The greatest covenant king of Yehudah accepts the prophecy of his descendants' exile with relief that it will not happen in his own lifetime.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 39 largely consistent with the Masoretic. This chapter corresponds to Melakhim Bet 20:12-19. The positioning of the Babylonian envoy narrative immediately after the Assyrian deliverance is the covenant rhetorical structure: the greatest deliverance in Yehudah's history is immediately followed by the announcement of its greatest disaster.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — he showed them his treasure house — there was nothing he did not show them. The same king who prayed with remarkable covenant faithfulness against Assyria responds to the Babylonian envoys with covenant pride. The prayer against Assyria was covenant dependence. The display to Babylon is covenant self-sufficiency. The two episodes are covenant mirrors: one reveals who Khizkiyahu is at his best; the other reveals his failure at his most prosperous. Verse 8 — good — for there will be peace in my days. The covenant response Yesha'yahu receives is the covenant antitype of Abraham's response to YHWH's call: Abraham left his comfort for covenant obligation. Khizkiyahu accepts the covenant disaster for his descendants with satisfaction that it exempts him personally.

THE BOOK OF CONSOLATION — CHAPTERS 40 THROUGH 66 — COMFORT MY PEOPLE

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 40 Comfort, Comfort My People — In the Wilderness Prepare the Way — Who Has Measured the Spirit of YHWH — They Shall Mount Up With Wings Like Eagles

1 Comfort, comfort my people, says your Creator.

2 Speak tenderly to Yerushalayim, and cry to her that her warfare is ended, that her iniquity is pardoned, that she has received from YHWH's hand double for all her sins.

3 A voice cries: In the wilderness prepare the way of YHWH — make straight in the desert a highway for our Creator.

4 Every valley shall be lifted up, and every mountain and hill be made low. The uneven ground shall become level, and the rough places a plain.

5 And the glory of YHWH shall be revealed, and all flesh shall see it together, for the mouth of YHWH has spoken.

6 A voice says, Cry. And I said, What shall I cry? All flesh is grass, and all its beauty is like the flower of the field.

7 The grass withers, the flower fades when the breath of YHWH blows on it — surely the people are grass.

8 The grass withers, the flower fades, but the word of our Creator will stand forever.

9 Go on up to a high mountain, O Tziyon, herald of good news — lift up your voice with strength, O Yerushalayim, herald of good news. Lift it up — fear not. Say to the cities of Yehudah, Behold your Creator.

10 Behold, the Lord YHWH comes with might, and his arm rules for him. Behold, his reward is with him, and his recompense before him.

11 He will tend his flock like a shepherd — he will gather the lambs in his arms. He will carry them in his bosom, and gently lead those that are with young.

12 Who has measured the waters in the hollow of his hand and marked off the heavens with a span, enclosed the dust of the earth in a measure and weighed the mountains in scales and the hills in a balance?

13 Who has measured the Spirit of YHWH, or what man shows him his counsel?

14 Whom did he consult, and who made him understand? Who taught him the path of justice, and taught him knowledge, and showed him the way of understanding?

15 Behold, the nations are like a drop from a bucket, and are accounted as the dust on the scales. Behold, he takes up the coastlands like fine dust.

16 Lebanon would not suffice for fuel, nor are its beasts enough for a burnt offering.

17 All the nations are as nothing before him — they are accounted by him as less than nothing and emptiness.

18 To whom then will you liken Creator, or what likeness compare with him?

19 An idol — a craftsman casts it, and a goldsmith overlays it with gold and casts for it silver chains.

20 He who is too impoverished for an offering chooses wood that will not rot. He seeks out a skillful craftsman to set up an idol that will not move.

21 Do you not know? Do you not hear? Has it not been told you from the beginning? Have you not understood from the foundations of the earth?

22 It is he who sits above the circle of the earth, and its inhabitants are like grasshoppers. Who stretches out the heavens like a curtain, and spreads them like a tent to dwell in.

23 Who brings princes to nothing, and makes the rulers of the earth as emptiness.

24 Scarcely are they planted, scarcely sown, scarcely has their stem taken root in the earth, when he blows on them, and they wither, and the tempest carries them off like stubble.

25 To whom then will you compare me, that I should be like him? says the Holy One.

26 Lift your eyes on high and see: who created these? He who brings out their host by number, calling them all by name. By the greatness of his might, and because he is strong in power, not one is missing.

27 Why do you say, O Yaakov, and speak, O Yisra'EL, My way is hidden from YHWH, and my right is disregarded by my Creator?

28 Have you not known? Have you not heard? YHWH is the everlasting Creator, the Creator of the ends of the earth. He does not faint or grow weary — his understanding is unsearchable.

29 He gives power to the faint, and to him who has no might he increases strength.

30 Even youths shall faint and be weary, and young men shall fall exhausted.

31 But they who wait for YHWH shall renew their strength — they shall mount up with wings like eagles. They shall run and not be weary — they shall walk and not faint.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 40 in full. Chapter 40 opens the second half of Yesha'yahu — sometimes called Deutero-Isaiah or the Book of Consolation — Chapters 40-66. 1QIsa-a shows no break, seam, or change of scribe between Chapter 39 and Chapter 40, treating the entire 66 chapters as a single unified scroll. This compilation follows 1QIsa-a in treating Yesha'yahu as a single book.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 40 in full. Verse 31 — they who wait for YHWH shall renew their strength — they shall mount up with wings like eagles — is one of the most beloved covenant promises in the Ethiopian canonical tradition, read as the covenant declaration of renewal for the covenant community exhausted by covenant suffering.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 40. Verse 8 — the grass withers, the flower fades, but the word of our Creator will stand forever — is the covenant declaration of prophetic authority that anchors the entire second half of Yesha'yahu: the prophet's word about the exile and restoration stands because it is YHWH's word.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1-2 — comfort, comfort my people — speak tenderly to Yerushalayim — her warfare is ended, her iniquity is pardoned, she has received double for all her sins. The opening of Chapter 40 is the covenant reversal of Chapter 1's opening indictment. Where Chapter 1 declared a sinful nation laden with iniquity, Chapter 40 declares iniquity pardoned and warfare ended. The covenant circle from judgment to comfort is the structural arc of the entire book. Verse 31 — they who wait for YHWH shall renew their strength. The covenant posture of waiting — the same posture of Chapters 8:17 and 26:8 — is now rewarded with covenant strength renewal. The eagle's flight is the covenant image of the exiled community's restoration.

YESHA'YAHU — CHAPTER 41 Fear Not I Am With You — The First and the Last — Who Stirred Up One From the East — I Am YHWH Your Creator

1 Listen to me in silence, O coastlands — let the peoples renew their strength. Let them approach — then let them speak. Let us together draw near for judgment.

2 Who stirred up one from the east whom victory meets at every step? He gives up nations before him, so that he tramples kings underfoot. He makes them like dust with his sword, like driven stubble with his bow.

3 He pursues them and passes on safely, by paths his feet have not trodden.

4 Who has performed and done this, calling the generations from the beginning? I, YHWH, the first, and with the last. I am he.

5 The coastlands have seen and are afraid — the ends of the earth tremble. They have drawn near and come.

6 Everyone helps his neighbor and says to his brother, Be strong.

7 The craftsman strengthens the goldsmith, and he who smooths with the hammer him who strikes the anvil, saying of the soldering, It is good. And they strengthen it with nails so that it cannot be moved.

8 But you, Yisra'EL, my servant, Yaakov, whom I have chosen, the offspring of Avraham, my friend —

9 You whom I took from the ends of the earth, and called from its farthest corners, saying to you, You are my servant — I have chosen you and not cast you off —

10 Fear not, for I am with you. Be not dismayed, for I am your Creator. I will strengthen you — I will help you — I will uphold you with my righteous right hand.

11 Behold, all who are incensed against you shall be put to shame and confounded. Those who strive against you shall be as nothing and shall perish.

12 You shall seek those who contend with you, but you shall not find them. Those who war against you shall be as nothing at all.

13 For I, YHWH your Creator, hold your right hand. It is I who say to you, Fear not — I am the one who helps you.

14 Fear not, you worm Yaakov, you men of Yisra'EL. I am the one who helps you, declares YHWH — your Redeemer is the Holy One of Yisra'EL.

15 Behold, I make of you a threshing sledge, new, sharp, and having teeth. You shall thresh the mountains and crush them, and you shall make the hills like chaff.

16 You shall winnow them, and the wind shall carry them away, and the tempest shall scatter them. And you shall rejoice in YHWH — in the Holy One of Yisra'EL you shall glory.

17 When the poor and needy seek water, and there is none, and their tongue is parched with thirst, I YHWH will answer them — I the Creator of Yisra'EL will not forsake them.

18 I will open rivers on the bare heights, and fountains in the midst of the valleys. I will make the wilderness a pool of water, and the dry land springs of water.

19 I will put in the wilderness the cedar, the acacia, the myrtle, and the olive. I will set in the desert the cypress, the plane and the pine together,

20 That they may see and know, may consider and understand together, that the hand of YHWH has done this, the Holy One of Yisra'EL has created it.

21 Set forth your case, says YHWH — bring your proofs, says the King of Yaakov.

22 Let them bring them, and tell us what is to happen. Tell us the former things, what they are, that we may consider them, that we may know their outcome. Or declare to us the things to come.

23 Tell us what is to come hereafter, that we may know that you are gods. Do good, or do harm, that we may be dismayed and terrified.

24 Behold, you are nothing, and your work is less than nothing. An abomination is he who chooses you.

25 I stirred up one from the north, and he has come, from the rising of the sun, and he shall call upon my name. He shall trample on rulers as on mortar, as the potter treads clay.

26 Who declared it from the beginning, that we might know, and beforehand, that we might say, He is right? There was none who declared it, none who proclaimed it, none who heard your words.

27 I was the first to say to Tziyon, Behold, here they are, and I give to Yerushalayim a herald of good news.

28 But when I look, there is no one — among these there is no counselor who, when I ask, gives an answer.

29 Behold, they are all a delusion — their works are nothing — their metal images are empty wind.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QIsa-a preserves Chapter 41 in full. The covenant lawsuit against the nations and the idols — verse 21-24 — is the first of the divine court speeches in the second half of Yesha'yahu. YHWH challenges the nations: declare what will happen, prove you can predict the future. The challenge goes unanswered. The covenant evidence of YHWH's unique deity is prophecy fulfilled.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 41 in full. Verse 10 — fear not, for I am with you — be not dismayed, for I am your Creator — I will strengthen you, I will help you, I will uphold you with my righteous right hand — is read as the covenant charter of the exiled community. The five covenant declarations — I am, I will strengthen, I will help, I will uphold — are the covenant promises that sustain the covenant community through exile.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 1QIsa-a and the Masoretic Text are in close agreement for Chapter 41. Verse 8-9 — you, Yisra'EL, my servant, Yaakov, whom I have chosen, the offspring of Avraham, my friend — you whom I took from the ends of the earth — I have chosen you and not cast you off — is the covenant foundation of the reassurance: the covenant election is not cancelled by the exile.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8-10 — you are my servant — I have chosen you and not cast you off — fear not, for I am with you. The covenant reassurance is addressed to a people in exile who might understandably conclude that YHWH has abandoned them. The covenant counter-declaration is threefold: I took you — I called you — I have chosen you and not cast you off. The covenant election is not revocable by covenant failure. The exile is covenant discipline, not covenant termination. Verse 14 — fear not, you worm Yaakov. The covenant address uses the most vulnerable, powerless image — a worm — for the covenant community in exile. YHWH does not address the exiles as if they are still powerful. He addresses their actual condition and declares his help for it.

END OF PART THREE — CHAPTERS 28 THROUGH 41
